title: Integrating Gene Synthesis and Microfluidics for Rapid Protein Engineering
author: Matthew Blackburn
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: The ability to rapidly design proteins with novel functions will have a significant impact on medicine, biotechnology, and synthetic biology. While synthetic genes are becoming a commodity, integrated approaches that take full advantage of gene synthesis have yet to be produced. We developed a bench-top, solid-phase gene synthesis method based on asymmetric primer extension (APE) and demonstrated that linear templates from this technique can be used directly for high-throughput, on-chip protein expression, purification, and quantitative characterization using MITOMI (mechanically induced trapping of molecular interactions). Gene synthesis by APE is a restriction enzyme- and ligase-free method that operates without the need for transformation, sequencing, or PCR/oligomer purification steps. By coupling APE with cell-free protein expression and MITOMI (APE-MITOMI), we completely circumvent any molecular cloning and cell-based steps, reducing the time between gene design and protein characterization to 3-4 days per prototyping cycle. With APE-MITOMI we synthesized and characterized over 400 Cys2His2 zinc-finger (ZF) transcription factors (TF), showing that although ZF TFs can be readily engineered to recognize a particular target DNA sequence, rationally engineering the precise binding energy landscape remains difficult. We also show that it is possible to tune ZF – DNA affinity precisely and independently of sequence specificity. The APE gene synthesis method can be readily automated on standard liquid handling robots, making it possible to characterize hundreds to thousands of synthetic proteins per week. APE-MITOMI is a generic approach that should facilitate fundamental studies in protein biophysics and protein design/engineering, and by enabling a detailed exploration of the protein sequence-function relationship, improve the accuracy of computational models of protein function.
